Items.Title: 使用 Azure 静态 Web 应用构建星球大战爬虫 - 第 3 部分 | Building Star Wars Crawler with Azure Static Web Apps - Part 3
Items.Description:
我们现在有我们的应用程序在本地运行，没有什么比把它提供给全世界更好的了，这样我们就可以向每个人展示我们开发的东西。

在这种情况下，我们将使用Azure 静态 Web 应用程序。

但首先，什么是 Azure 静态 Web 应用？ 你可以看到围绕 ASWA 的整个工作流程：

我们将使用扩展直接在 Visual Studio Code 中部署我们的应用程序：

  • 打开 VS Code，让我们直接进入 Azure 门户！ 单击 Azure 图标并使用您的 Azure 帐户登录：

    使用+号转到Create Static Web App ：

    现在，请按照以下步骤操作：

    1. 包括应用名称。 例如：intro-starwars-app
    2. 选择一个地区来托管我们的应用程序。
    3. 现在，选择构建预设来配置默认项目结构。 在这种情况下，我们不使用框架。 只是 JavaScript、HTML 和 CSS。 在这种情况下选择： custom
    4. 现在我们需要包括我们的应用程序的位置。 我们的应用程序将是： /public
    5. 最后一步是包含我们构建输出的位置。 在这种情况下，删除build并留空

    将打开一个框，单击链接： Open Actions in GitHub

    单击它后，您将被重定向到显示整个构建工作流程（CI 和 CD）的 GitHub Actions 存储库页面。

    现在让我们回到 Azure 静态 Web 应用程序扩展并右键单击Browser Site

    并查看应用程序的运行情况！

    如果您愿意，您可以在此处查看已部署的应用程序 - Star Wars Intro App

    最后的话

    今天我们学习了如何使用 HTML、CSS 和 JavaScript/Node.js 创建星球大战介绍应用程序。 最后，我们使用 Azure 静态 Web 应用服务托管了我们的应用程序。

    在这里，我想提供一些关于 Azure 静态 Web 应用程序和 GitHub 操作的好资源：

    并且要保持在其他几个新闻的顶部，请务必在 Twitter 上关注我！

    并订阅我的Youtube 频道 - Glaucia Lemos ，每周观看有关 Web 开发、Node.js 和 JavaScript 的新视频！

    再见！ 😍 😍



原文:

We have our application working locally now, nothing better than making it available to the world so that we can show everyone what we have developed.

In this case we are going to use Azure Static Web Apps.

But firstly, what is Azure Static Web Apps? Azure Static Web Apps is a service that automatically builds and deploys full stack web apps to Azure from a code repository. Belo you can see the whole workflow around the ASWA:

And we're going to deploy our application using directly in the Visual Studio Code using the extension:

  • Visual Studio Code Extension - Azure Static Web Apps

Open up VS Code and let's get into the Azure Portal right there! Click on the Azure icon and sign in with your Azure account:

Go to the Create Static Web App with a + sign:

Now, follow these steps:

  1. Include a app name. Ex.: intro-starwars-app
  2. Select a region for hosting our app.
  3. Now, choose the build preset to configure default project structure. In this case we are not using frameworks. Just JavaScript, HTML and CSS. In this case choose: custom
  4. Now we need to include the location of our application. For our application will be: /public
  5. The final step is to include the location of our build output. In this case, remove build and leave blank

A box will open, click on the link: Open Actions in GitHub

Once you click it, you will be redirected to the GitHub Actions repository page showing the entire build workflow (C.I and C.D).

Now let's return to the Azure Static Web Apps Extension and right click on Browser Site

And see the app in action!

If you want, you can see the deployed application HERE - Star Wars Intro App

Final Words

Today we have learned how to create a Star Wars Intro App using: HTML, CSS and JavaScript/Node.js. And finally we hosted our application using the Azure Static Web Apps service.

Here I would like to let some good resources about Azure Static Web Apps & GitHub Actions:

And to stay on top of several other news, be sure to follow me on Twitter!

And subscribe on my Youtube Channel - Glaucia Lemos to see weekly new videos about Web Development, Node.js & JavaScript!

See you! 😍
Items.Author: glaucia86

Items.Title: 我作为 Javascript 软件工程师的 10 年经验 | My 10 Years Experience as a Javascript Software Engineer
Items.Description:
我喜欢总是用一个漂亮的帖子来象征重要的事件，这让我有机会停下来真正思考我必须克服的众多事件和挑战才能实现这个重要的里程碑。 当某人开始新的职业生涯时，通常感觉就像是跳入了未知世界，因为我们完全不知道未来几年会发生什么。

在这篇文章中，我将分享我的感受、成功和失败，希望这能帮助您明确自己的道路。 诚然，没有一个故事是相同的，你的道路和职业可能与我的非常不同，但这篇文章的目的是强调我目前强烈的自尊心背后实际上是大量的失败和不确定性。

在我开始之前——10 多年前

如果你在任何社交网络上关注我或在任何社交活动中加入我，你就会知道我作为开发人员的职业生涯实际上并没有计划好。 在我的开发生涯真正开始之前，我花了十几岁的时间攻读商业管理和经济学学位，并拥有自己的咖啡馆 18 个月。

当时，互联网上还没有#100daysOfDevs 之类的东西，我所在的新兵训练营也不是很普遍，所以我对成为一名自我思考的开发者的期望非常有限。 我不会详细介绍，因为这不是这篇文章的范围，但你应该知道的是，我设法获得了我的第一份开发人员工作，但技术知识和经验非常有限（几个月的 YouTube 和晚上的副业）。

如果您目前正在努力获得您的第一份开发人员工作，您可能已经在说我很幸运，事情不再以这种方式工作了。 但作为一个帮助许多人找到第一份开发人员工作并且仍然大量参与面试的人，我可以保证这仍然是可能的。 当我回头看那个年轻的我时，我可以看到仍然在我的血管中流淌着强烈的东西——激情！

多年前的我，与我目前面试或帮助的学徒之间的相似之处在于愿意成功。 如果你必须从这篇文章中得到一些东西，那么你的实际技能和投资组合只是获得工作（以及你职业生涯的进步）所需的一小部分，那就是学习的意愿，热情成功和接受批评的能力。 永远不要因为“你就是那个人”，或者因为“你知道所有要知道的事情”而期望公司提供这份工作，因为我可以向你保证，你会惨遭失败。 我不是说你不够了解，但我可以说有很多东西要学，10年我个人只是摸到了皮毛，所以你几个月的学习肯定比不上（或至少不是一个好的态度开始🙂）

初级开发商——镇上最大的冒名顶替者

我仍然清楚地记得，当我作为开发人员的第一天走到我的办公室时，我的腿不受控制地颤抖着。 在去办公室的路上，我忍不住想，我是个大骗子，我通过愚弄面试官设法得到了这份工作，但我实际上不知道我打算做什么，如果我能甚至当天完成了一个简单的任务。

不幸的是，这种感觉并不容易真正消除，它伴随着我很长一段时间。 无论你工作多么努力，学了多少，科技行业是如此广泛，以至于你很容易发现自己在交谈或从事超出你习惯的任务，冒名顶替者的感觉会以你想象不到的速度重新出现。

如果您目前正在发生这种情况，我可以向您保证这是完全正常的，并且您不是冒名顶替者。 一家公司从不为您提供机会，因为他们希望您为日常工作做好准备，但从长远来看，您将能够提供什么，这是对您是谁的投资，而不是您所知道的现在！ 花时间学习，努力工作，随着时间的推移，你将能够控制这种感觉。

不幸的是，冒名顶替综合症实际上是你可以摆脱的，但它会在你的职业生涯中一直伴随着你。 我个人仍然觉得自己每天工作都是一个冒名顶替者，但正如我之前所说，随着时间的推移，你将能够“控制这种感觉”，并将其作为一种武器来集中学习和提高自己。

有很多关于冒名顶替综合症的帖子和书籍，我强烈建议您在开始职业生涯之前先了解一下。 您永远不会做好充分的准备，但了解它可能会帮助您减轻这种感觉！ 我发现 Codementor 的这篇博客文章是一个很好的阅读资源： https ://www.codementor.io/blog/imposter-syndrome-deypemtirw

仍然是一家初级的新公司

自从我第一次经历以来，仅仅 6 个月过去了，我开始为我的下一个职位做好准备。 作为一个新的开发者，你会开始觉得自己立于不败之地，并认为在你现在的位置上没有什么可以学习的（或者我一开始就是这么觉得的），所以我决定继续前进，在我的职业生涯中取得进步。

下图准确表示了你的职业生涯将如何发展（或至少是内部感觉）：

当我找到一个新职位并开始我的新工作时，我意识到我只知道我所接触到的东西，而这根本不知道。 在我的第一次体验中，我只是习惯于定义静态 HTML 和 CSS（没有表单或什么都没有的简单），但在我的新职位上，所使用的技术范围扩大了 10 倍以上，我发现自己又回到了那种不确定性腿颤抖的区域。

上面的图表不会只出现一次，它们会在你的职业生涯中一遍又一遍地出现。 下降到“令人沮丧的现实”，听起来很可怕，实际上将成为你下一个职业发展的起点。 正如聪明的苏格拉底曾经说过的：

中型开发人员 – 众多开发人员之一

在本节中，我们快进了几年。 我在同一家公司工作了 6 年多，因此本节和以下部分都将在同一家公司进行。 即使我在同一家公司工作，公司的成长和不同的项目仍然为我提供了很多机会来经历上面分享的学习周期。

在这个阶段，我开始控制自己对未知的恐惧，并对失败抱有良好的态度。 在我生命的这个阶段，主要问题是我只是普通人，我只是“众多”其他开发人员中的一个。 从“幼稚自信的山丘”走下来会让你意识到自己并不特别，这对像我这样有竞争力的人来说是一个很大的打击。

成为一名普通的开发人员并每天工作以完成您的目标并没有错。 我遇到了许多出色的开发人员，他们很高兴来到这个地方并且没有前进的动力（除非由于时间原因）。 我走了一条非常不同的道路，我朝着一个更大的目标努力，我给自己设定了一项非常艰巨且无法完成的任务，我日复一日地努力工作以取得成功。

我还记得在我 20 多岁的时候，我和妻子坐下来，在谈话中我和她分享了我的计划。 那时我才2年左右的经验，很清楚自己还是个大三，还有很多要学的，但我告诉妻子“5年后，我要做公司的CTO” .

回首过去并与现在相比，我可以很容易地看出我非常天真（即使我实现了大部分目标，我们稍后会看到），但那次小小的谈话是我现在的基础成功。 这个承诺的作用，为我的学习策略奠定了基础，它为我提供了在失败后站起来所必需的力量。

在接下来的几年里，我专注于我不知道的事情，向同龄人学习，倾听和做笔记。 我一遍又一遍地重复上述学习周期。 我不只是做我的工作，我也看看我的上司做了什么，我要求积极的反馈，我抓住机会向前迈进，如前所述，我失败了很多次，但坚持不懈也会带来成功。

这条我称之为“快车道”的路并不简单，也不是每个人都应该走的路。 但是，即使您没有以与我相同的速度执行此操作，您可能仍想以此为例说明您需要做些什么才能进一步进步。 确实，4-5 年后，你可能会被称为大四（这就是我上面所说的，当你因为经验而前进时），但事实是，如果你真的想前进，如果你想感觉像一个高级并消除您内心的那种冒名顶替的感觉，您必须掌握自己的技能，您必须突破自己的界限并一遍又一遍地这样做。

由于科技行业的性质，成为一名开发人员并不容易，但成为一名优秀的开发人员更难。 长话短说，在不到 5 年的经验中，我成功地成为了一名技术主管，并照顾了 40 多名开发人员。 我通过努力工作和奉献精神做到了这一点，你也可以。

高级开发人员——控制我的弱点

我们现在已经进入我的职业生涯 5 年（中途），我现在是所谓的高级开发人员（我感觉自己是一名并且被称为一名），但我还有很长的路要走。 技术职业并不全是编码，在职业阶梯中向前发展，将开始暴露出更少“技术性”和更多“管理性”的技能。 您的专业知识将较少受到您的编码能力的驱动，而更多地取决于您的领导技能。

当我成为技术主管时，我广泛团队中的许多人比我更有经验，还有许多人比我更专业，所以我又开始觉得自己像个冒名顶替者（是的，我要学习更多）。 对我来说幸运的是，我有机会得到伟大的经理的支持，他们真正看到了我，我还没有看到，并一路支持我。

随着时间的推移，我学到的是，我有机会领导别人的主要原因与我的技术技能无关，而是我对更广泛的团队和学习的态度。 这个范围有点太宽了，不能只用一小章来介绍，但我会给你一个小小的见解，让你了解我所学到的成功领导的秘诀：

要谦虚

我在上一章中没有提到这一点，因为我认为在这里这样做更有意义，但谦虚从第一天起就对我有所帮助，现在仍然如此。 我从不对自己的能力撒谎，我对自己知道的和不知道的非常清楚。 这不仅清楚地定义了您对其他人的身份，而且还为您提供了一个干净的石板，让您能够寻求帮助并寻求新的学习机会

接受别人的技能

我见过许多高级开发人员过于固执，无法接受错误或不知道某事。 在我的职业生涯中，我实际上做了相反的事情，无论我的职位如何。 实际上，我一直称赞他人的专长，并公开否定我对一位同行（有时甚至是初级）的建议/解决方案。 这听起来可能与前一点相似，但这只是它的结果。 在上一个，我很乐意接受自己的弱点，在这里我很乐意接受其他的优点。

分享你的失败

世界上到处都是超级英雄（感谢奇迹）。 社交媒体平台上充满了成功的伟大故事（也许像这个），但相信我一直非常清楚自己的失败。 我很高兴分享我只是一个人，这真的帮助我打破了我和我的同事之间的墙。

代表

我在之前的故事中已经稍微提到了这一点，但我想强调授权的重要性。 你的大脑只是一块肌肉，你无法完全学习通过你的方式的所有东西，或者即使你有可用的记忆来学习任何要知道的东西，也可能没有足够的时间让你这样做.

知道这两种资源是有限的，通过尝试委派可能对您不重要的事情并专注于真正重要的事情来解决它们是非常重要的。

为你的听众说话

当您成为开发人员时，您很可能会被称为“书呆子”。

我使用上述描述来解释与您周围的人有效沟通（面对面或通过电子邮件）所必需的技能。 无论我有什么会议或谈话，我总是问自己这两个问题：

  • 对话另一端的人知道我在说什么吗？
  • 对话另一方的人是否关心我正在分享的主题？

如果以上任何一个问题的答案是否定的，那么你现在应该停下来，尝试改变你表达自己的方式，以更符合你周围的人。 如果你参加管理会议，永远不要谈论错误和代码异味，而是将其转化为“维护成本”和“代码质量”，如果你是在与初级开发人员交谈，不要使用复杂的术语，而是提供简单的例子来解释情况。

我很幸运能够进入一所这样认为的管理学院，我可以清楚地说这是一项无价的资产。

概括

在我们进入下一章之前，我将再分享几句话，以提供一个包含上述所有要点的真实示例。 在我最初担任技术主管的时候，我一次又一次地受到挑战（并不是所有的前辈都对我的职位感到满意），而且我脑海中只有一次会议：

我们正在召开一个有许多经理和资深人士参加的架构会议。 在这次会议上，我被问到一个具体的问题，并要求我提出解决问题的办法。 相反，比起在我的头上给他们一个解决方案，我明确地定义了我更愿意让一个经验不足的团队成员在这里帮助我们（中间开发人员，所以不参加本次会议）。 我的公开求助遇到了一位高级主管，他向我的经理表示我不应该担任那个职位，因为我无法“履行”对我来说必要的角色。 正如你可能想象的那样，我感到无助并且完全无法控制（是的，我的腿开始颤抖，但幸运的是我坐着）。 我唯一的感觉是彻底失败，我认为我真的是一个冒名顶替者，我不应该在那里。 但幸运的是（正如我之前所说，我有一位出色的经理），我的首席运营官实际上接任并回答说：“西蒙尼实际上是这份工作的合适人选，因为他可以接受自己的弱点，而不是相反”。 这对我来说是一个大开眼界的时刻，我会在余生中珍惜这一刻。

飞跃——软件架构师

我意识到这篇文章越来越长，但 10 年就是 10 年。 所以我要向前跳一点。 在上一章中，我正在享受我作为技术主管的新力量，并努力学习在该职位上取得成功所必需的许多新技能，但现在已经过去了很多年，我决定继续前进。 正如我们在搬家之前了解到的那样，当你被弹跳到“令人沮丧的现实”时，可能会非常痛苦，但我已经做好了准备，并为新的挑战感到高兴。

在我的公司工作了 6 年并担任了大约 4 年的技术主管后，我决定继续前进并加入了我目前在 This Dot 担任软件架构师的职位（我在受雇时不是一名架构师，但几个月后被提升为一名架构师）。

在这里，我拥有 7 年的经验和一长串以前的成功，再次害怕新的挑战！ 到现在为止，我一直在威尔士的当地企业工作，搬到一家国际公司后，我内心出现了一系列新的“不安全感”……。

如果威尔士的人们实际上不如人们监督的那么好怎么办

我将如何获得远程工作的帮助，我将如何应用我现有的技能

上面的想法和更多的想法在我脑海里盘旋了好几个月，直到我终于开始。 那一刻我开始意识到一些事情（当然，资历也有帮助），无论我将面临什么样的困难和挑战，最重要的是成功的意愿。

您可能还记得上面这句话，事实上，这就是我用来解释什么是初级开发人员角色的好候选人的同一句话！ 当人们申请第一份工作时，我个人在他们眼中看到的同样的火花，也是我多年前所拥有的火花，是你成功所需的唯一真正技能！

自从加入 This Dot 以来，我遇到了令人惊叹的开发人员，他们的简历让我的简历看起来毫无用处，在伟大的公司工作并交付了许多伟大的项目。 我时不时还在经历“冒名顶替综合症”，这完全没有问题，但我 10 年的经历让我大开眼界，让我意识到，无论遇到什么挑战，迟早我都会成功。

结论

我们现在已经到了这篇文章的结尾。 我在一个帖子中分享了许多“颤抖腿”、“失败”、巨大弱点和持续挫折的例子。 正如许多人所说，人们通常将成功视为运气和或给予的，但我个人从无到有，一路努力。 我为此努力工作，我不只是等待未来落地，这是有代价的（无休止的学习时间和在我的会议区之外不断工作）。

最近，我写了一本书，在我的职业生涯中实现了另一个里程碑，我希望在我刚开始我的职业生涯时就拥有这本书。 如果您喜欢上面的帖子，我建议您获得一份副本（只需 5 美元，使用以下代码 - https://leanpub.com/beyond-coding/c/10-years-post ）。

是时候说再见了，继续我的职业生涯 10 年，看看这会给我带来什么。 我现在明白了，你永远不会到达终点，而每一个转折点，都有一个新的开始。 愿你的事业和我的一样富有成效，如果我可以给你任何帮助，请联系我，如果你觉得这篇文章有用，请在下面发表评论。



原文:

I like to always symbolize important events with a nice post, this gives me the chance to stop and really think about the numerous events and challenges that I had to surpass to be able to achieve this big milestone. When someone starts a new career, it usually feels like a jump into the unknown, as we are completely unaware of what will happen in the years to pass.

In this post, I am going to share my feeling, successes and failures, with the hope that this will help you give some clarity to your own path. It is true that no story is the same, and your paths and career may be very different from mine, but the aim of this post is to highlight what lies behind my current strong self-esteem is actually a multitude of failures and uncertainty.

Before I even started – Over 10 years ago

If you follow me on any social network or joined me at any social event, you will already know that my career as a developer was not actually planned. I spend my teens studying toward a Business Management and Economics degree and own my own cafe for 18 months before my career in development actually started.

At the time, the internet did not have things such as #100daysOfDevs, and boot camps were not very common where I come from, so my expectation of becoming a self-thought developer was very limited. I am not going to go into many details as this is not the scope of this post, but what you should know is that I managed to get my first ever developer job with very limited tech knowledge and experience (a couple of months of YouTube and side projects in the evenings).

If you are currently working to get your first developer job, you are probably already saying that I have been lucky and that things do not work this way anymore. But as someone that has helped many people find their first developer job and is still heavily involved in interviews, I can guarantee that this is still possible. When I look back at that young me, I can see something that still flows strong into my vein – Passion!

The similarities between me many years ago, and the apprentices that I currently interview or help is the willingness to succeed. If there is something that you have to get out of this post is this, your actual skillset and portfolio are just a very small portion of what is needed to get a job (and progress in your career) is the willingness to learn, the passion to succeed and ability to accept criticism. Never go into an interview expecting a company to give the job because “you are the guy”, or because “you know all that there is to know” because I can guarantee you that you will fail miserably. I am not saying that you do not know enough, but I can say that there is so much to learn that in 10 years I have personally just scratched the surface, so your few months of learning can surely not be a match (or at least is not a good attitude to start with 🙂 )

Junior developer – the biggest imposter in town

I still remember vividly that while I walked to my office for my first day as a developer, my legs were shaking uncontrollably. During the journey to the office, I could not stop but to think that I was a BIG imposter and that I managed to get the job by fooling the interviewer, but that I actually had no idea what I was meant to do and if I could even complete a simple task that day.

Unfortunately, this feeling is not an easy feeling to actually remove and it stayed with me for quite a long time. No matter how hard you work and how much you learn, the tech industry is so wide that you will easily find yourself in conversation or in tasks that are beyond your conform and the feeling of imposter will pop back in faster than you can even imagine.

If this is currently happening to you, I can guarantee you that it is totally normal and that you are not an imposter. A company never provides you with an opportunity because they expect you to be ready for your day to day job, but because of what you will be able to provide in the long run, it is an investment in who you are, not what you know right now! Take the time to learn, work hard and with time you will be able to control this feeling.

Unfortunately, Imposter Syndrome will actually be something that you can get rid of, but it will stay with you for the rest of your career. I personally still feel like an imposter on a daily basis on my job, but as I said before, with time you will be able to “control this feeling” and use it as a weapon to focus your learning and improve yourself.

There are many posts and books that cover imposter syndrome and I greatly suggest you get to know it a bit before you get started in your career. You will never be fully prepared, but the knowledge of it may help you alleviate the feeling! I found this blog post from Codementor to be a lovely resource to read: https://www.codementor.io/blog/imposter-syndrome-deypemtirw

Still a junior, new company

Just 6 months have passed since my first ever experience, and I started to feel ready for my next position. As a new developer, you will start to feel invincible, and think that there is nothing else for you to learn in your current position (or so I felt at the very start), so I decided to move on and progress in my career.

The following graph is a precise representation of how your career will work out (or at least the internal feeling):

https://medium.com/@victorcodes/flattening-the-learning-curve-as-a-software-developer-25b7954d84daIt was my first job, I got carried away and was naive with myself in expecting to know it all! When I found a new position and started my new job, I realised that I just knew what I was exposed to and that was not much at all. In my very first experience, I was just used to defining static HTML and CSS (simple ones with no form or nothing), but in my new position the scope of the technologies used grew more than 10 folds and I found myself back to that uncertainty zone with shaky legs.

The above graphs won’t just happen once, but they will happen over and over again in your career. The drop to “discouragingly realistic”, as scary as it sounds, is actually going to be the starting point to your next career growth. As the wise Socrates once said:

https://www.azquotes.com/quote/667157Finding things that you do not know and working to put them into practice, is the drive you need to succeed and improve.

Mid Developer – One of many

In this section, we are fast-forwarding a couple of years. I spent over 6 years in the same company, so this and the following sections will all take place in the same company. Even if I worked in the same company, the company growth and different projects have still provided me with many opportunities to go through the learning cycle that has been shared above.

At this stage, I was starting to control my fear of the unknown and have a good attitude toward failure. The main problem during this phase of my life was that I was just average, I was just “one of many” other developers. Coming down from the “hill of naive confidence” makes you realise that you are not special, and that is a big blow for someone as competitive as me.

There is nothing wrong with being an average developer and working your day to complete your goals. I have meet many fantastic developers that are happy to be in this place and have no incentive to move forward (unless due to time). I took a very different path, I worked toward a greater goal, I set myself a very hard and unachievable task and I worked hard day in and day out to succeed.

I still remember back in my mid-20s, I sat down with my wife and during a conversation I share my plan with her. At this point, I had just 2 years or so of experience and was well aware that I was still a junior and had lots to learn, but I told my wife “In 5 years, I a going to be the CTO of the company”.

Looking back at where I was and comparing it to where I am now, I can easily see that I was extremely naive (even if I achieved most of my goals as we will see later), but that small conversation was the base of my current success. What that promise did, was set the foundation for my learning strategy, it provided me with the strength necessary to stand up after failures.

I spent the next few years of my career focusing on what I did not know, learning from my peers, listening and taking notes. I repeated the above learning cycle over and over. I did not just do my work, but I also looked at what my superior did, I requested active feedback, I took my chances to move forward and as already mentioned I failed many times, but with perseverance also comes success.

This path that I call the “fast track” is not simple, and it is not what everyone is supposed to follow. But even if you do not do this at the same speed that I did, you may still want to take this as an example of what will be needed of you to progress further. It is true that after 4-5 years you may be called a senior (that is what I meant above when you move forward because of experience), but the truth is that if you REALLY want to move forward if you want to feel like a senior and remove that feeling of imposter within you, you have to master your skills, you have to push your boundaries and do this over and over.

Being a developer is not easy due to the nature of the tech industry, but being a good developer is even harder. Long story short I managed to become a tech lead and looked after over 40 developers in less than 5 years of experience. I did so with hard work and dedication and you can too.

Senior Developer – In control of my weakness

We are now 5 years into my career (halfway) and I am now what is called a senior developer (I felt like one and was called one), but I still had a long way to go. The tech career is not all around coding, and moving forward in the career ladder, will start to expose skills that are less “technical” and more “managerial”. Your expertise will be less driven by your ability to code, and more by your skill to lead.

When I became the tech lead, many people within my wide team were more experienced than me, and many others were more specialised than me, so I started to feel like an imposter again (yes, more learning to come for me). Luckily for me, I had the opportunity to be supported by great managers that really saw in me, what I could not see yet, and supported me all the way.

What I learned over time was that the main reason why I was given the opportunity to lead people was not related to my technical skills, but my attitude toward the wider team and learning. The scope of this is a bit too wide to just cover in a small chapter, but I am going to give you a small insight into what I have learned to have been my recipe for successful leading:

Be humble

I have not mentioned this in the previous chapter as I think it makes more sense to do it here, but being humble has helped me from my very first day and still does now. I never lie about my ability, and I am super clear about what I know and what I do not know. This not only clearly defined who you are to others, but it also provides a clean slate for you to be able to ask for help and reach out for new learning opportunities

Accept other people’s skills

I have seen many senior developers being too stubborn to accept being wrong and or accept not knowing something. In my career, I have actually done the opposite, no matter my position. I have actually always praised others’ speciality and openly trashed my suggestion/solution for one of my peers (at times even junior). This may sound similar to the previous point, but is just a consequence of it. In the previous one, I was happy to accept my weakness, here I am happy to accept other strengths.

Share your failure

The world is full of superheroes (thanks to marvel). Social media platforms are full of great stories of success (like this one maybe), but the trust is that I have always been very clear about my failure. I am happy to share that I am only a human and this really helped me to break up the wall between me and my colleagues.

Delegate

I have covered this point slightly above in my previous story, but I want to emphasise the importance of delegating. Your brain is just a muscle and there is no way for you to learn absolutely everything that passes your way, or even if you have the available memory to learn anything that there is to know, there may not be enough time for you to do so.

Knowing that these two resources are finite, it is very important that you work around them by trying to delegate what may not be important to you and focus on what really matters.

Speak for your audience

As you become a developer, chances are that you will be called a “nerd”.

https://www.geekwire.com/2013/study-women-choose-computer-science-careers-nerd-stereotypes/If this happens it does not mean that you look anywhere close to the above image, or that you have anything wrong, what this means is that you are not speaking the language of the people that are around you, and have bored them with things that they have absolutely no interest in.

I use the above description to explain the skills necessary to communicate effectively with people around you (either face to face or by email). No matter what meeting or conversation I have, I always ask myself these two questions:

  • Does the person on the other side of this conversation know what I am talking about?
  • Does the person on the other side of this conversation care about the subject that I am sharing?

If the answer is no to any of the questions above, then you should stop right now and try to change the way you express yourself to be more in line with the people around you. If you are attending a management meeting never talk about bugs and code smells, but turn this into “maintenance costs” and “code quality”, if you are talking with a junior developer do not use the complex terms, but provide simple examples to explain the situation.

I have been fortunate to have attended a management school that has thought me this and I can clearly say that it has been an invaluable asset.

Summary

Before we move to the next chapter, I am going to share just a few more words to provide a real-life example that encapsulates all the points above. In my initial time as tech lead I was challenged over and over (not all the seniors were happy with my position), and there is a single meeting that is clear in my mind:

We were having an architecture meeting attended by many managers and seniors. During this meeting, I was asked a specific question and require to come up with a solution to a problem. Instead, than give them a solution on top of my head, I clearly defined that I would prefer for a less experienced team member to help us here (mid developer so not part of this current meeting). My public call for help was confronted by a senior denoting to my managers that I was not meant to be in that position as I could not “fulfil” the role that was necessary for me. As you may imagine, I felt helpless and totally not in control (yes my legs started to shake, but I was sitting luckily). The only feeling I had was of total failure, I thought I was really an imposter and I should not have been there. But luckily (as I said before I had a great manager), my COO actually took over and replied “Simone is actually the right person for this job because he can accept his weakness, not the other way around”. This was a great eye-opening moment for me, I will cherish that moment for the rest of my life.

The big leap – Software Architect

I am realising that this post is getting very long, but 10 years are 10 years. So I am going to jump a little forward. In the previous chapter, I was enjoying my new power as a tech lead and worked toward many new skills necessary to succeed in that position, but the years have now passed and I have decided to move on. As we learned before moving company can be pretty traumatic as you are catapulted down to the “discouragingly realistic”, but I was ready for it and happy for a new challenge.

After 6 years in my company and around 4 as a tech lead, I decided to move forward and joined my current position as a Software Architect in This Dot (I was not an architect when I was employed but got promoted to one after a few months).

Here I am, with 7 years of experience and a long list of previous successes, scared again of a new challenge! Until now I have worked in local businesses around Wales, and moving to an international company opened a new set of “insecurity” within me….

What if people in Wales are actually not as good as the people oversees

How will I get help working remotely, how will I apply my existing skills

The above thoughts and many more twisted and turned in my head for many months until I finally started. At that moment I have started to realise something (of course seniority also helps here), that no matter the difficulties and challenges that I will be provided with, what matters the most is the willingness to succeed.

You may remember the above sentence, in fact, that was the same sentence I used to explain what makes a good candidate for a junior developer role! That same spark that I personally see in people’s eyes when they apply for their first job, the same one that I had many years ago, is the one and only real skill you need to succeed!

Since joining This Dot I have met amazing developers with CVs that make mine look useless, worked in great companies and delivered many great projects. I am still experiencing “imposter syndrome” from time to time and this is totally fine, but my 10 years of experience have opened my eyes and made me realise that no matter the challenges, in time, I will succeed.

Conclusion

We have now reached the end of this post. A post in which I have shared numerous instances of “shaking legs”, “failure”, great weakness and ongoing setbacks. As many people say, people usually see success as luck and or given, but I personally started from nothing and worked my way up. I worked hard for it, I did not just wait for the future to land on my legs, and that comes with a price (endless hours of learning and constantly working outside of my conference zone).

I have recently achieved another milestone in my career by writing a book that I wished I had when I first started my career. If you enjoyed the above post I suggest you get a copy (It is just 5$ with the following code – https://leanpub.com/beyond-coding/c/10-years-post ).

It is time to say goodbye and continue my career for another 10 years and see where that brings me. I have learned now that you never reach the end, and at every turn, there is a new beginning. May your career be as fruitful as mine and please reach out if there is any help that I can give you or comment below if you found this post useful.
Items.Author: zelig880

Items.Title: React 状态管理 - 第 1 部分：简介和本地状态管理 | React state management - Part 1: Introduction & local state management
Items.Description:
在使用 React 构建网站时，您将大量使用和依赖的最重要的事情之一就是管理您的状态。

在本系列中，我将说明在我的应用程序和网站中管理状态的最佳用例和方法。

什么是反应？

十多年前，开发者习惯于使用 Vanilla JS 构建网站，同时编写大量冗余代码，导致流程较长。 几年后，开发人员能够使用新引入的基于 UI 组件的 JS 框架。 这使得制作现代网站的过程更容易、更短，并具有额外的功能，例如重用我们网站上的任何组件。 什么是 React 状态管理？

每个 React 组件都有一个内置的状态对象，您可以在其中存储在组件渲染之间持久的数据/资产。 我们可以在本地或使用外部状态管理库管理任何组件状态。

本地状态管理

在本系列的这一部分中，我们将讨论在本地管理我们的状态。

虽然 React 附带了许多有用的钩子，但使用最广泛的钩子之一是useState()钩子。

首先，到底什么是钩子？ 在官方文档中，Hook 是一个特殊的函数，可以让你挂钩 React 特性。 示例 #1

假设我们有一个按钮，用户可以切换以显示/隐藏特定数据。 为了保持这个按钮的状态，我们必须使用useState钩子来跟踪状态本身。 让我们看看下面的例子：

 import {useState} from 'react' export default function App() { // Declare a new state variable, which we'll call "isVisible" // It could be of any type, but here its a Boolean const [isVisible, setIsVisible] = useState(false) return ( <> <button onClick={setIsVisible(prev => !prev)}>Toggle</button> {isVisible && <div>Hello there my friends, again!!</div>} </> ) }

首先，当我们调用 useState 钩子时，我们声明了一个新的“状态变量”。 它返回一对值：当前状态变量，在我们的示例中是isVisible ，以及我们必须使用来更新此值的函数，在我们的示例中也称为setIsVisible 。 我们传递给钩子本身的参数是初始状态，在我们的示例中表示布尔值 (false)。

接下来，我们使用回调函数挂钩我们的按钮，调用setIsVisible(prev => !prev) 。 单击此按钮将有效地将此布尔值翻转为与其先前值相反的值。

因此，当用户第一次按下切换按钮时，它会将isVisible布尔值从 false 更改为 true，从而显示消息<div>Hello there my friends, again!!</div> 。 再次单击它会导致再次像原来一样隐藏该值。

示例 #2

现在让我们再举一个例子：

 import {useState} from 'react' const Message = ({user}) => { return ( <h1>User Name: {user.name}</h1> <h2>User Age: {user.age}<h2> ) } export default function App() { const [user, setUser] = useState({ name: 'Sam', age: '24' }) return ( <> <p>User information:</p> <Message user={user} > </div> ) }

在上面的示例中，在 App 组件（Parent）中，我们使用useState钩子声明了一个新的状态变量user ，同时将初始状态作为包含一些用户信息的对象传递（模仿从一些 auth API 获取的对象）。 然后我们调用我们现在将创建的子组件Message并将user状态变量作为道具传递。 然后我们创建这个Message子组件并从 props 中解构用户状态变量并根据需要使用它（在我们的示例中，我们只是在一个简单的消息中显示了用户的姓名和年龄）。

这个例子只是一个关于状态变量如何从父组件传递到其子组件的非常简单和基本的演示，这与 prop-drilling 相关。 虽然我们应该始终记住，这种数据流始终是向下的，这意味着我们根本无法在子组件中声明状态变量并在父组件中使用它，但反过来也是如此。

螺旋钻的缺点

在更复杂的开发环境中，有时对某些状态进行简单的钻探可能会给我们的工作流程带来复杂性。 原因是如果我们需要不直接在子级中使用某个状态变量，而是在更深的嵌套子级中使用。 我们需要调整我们的代码并找到一种更好的方式来使用来自祖父母（祖父母）的状态。 这正是状态管理库发挥作用的地方。 在本博客系列的以下部分中，我们将看看我在处理全局状态管理时的实现。

最后的想法

状态管理在任何 Web 开发架构中都是强制性的，因此掌握处理状态管理的最佳实践和方法将极大地促进您的 Web 开发事业，因此请务必阅读本博客系列的下一部分。

我一直在撰写新的博客文章，在其中分享我在与 Web 开发相关的主题方面的专业知识和技能。 如果您对此类主题感兴趣以促进您的发展事业，请考虑关注我！ 谢谢你的阅读，Ciao！ 👋 👋



原文:

While building a website using React, one of the most important things that you'll heavily use and rely upon is managing your state.

Throughout this series, I'll illustrate on the best use cases and ways to manage state throughout my applications and websites.

What is React?

More than a decade ago, developers used to build websites using Vanilla JS, while writing a lot of redundant code, resulting in longer processes. After a couple of years, developers were able to use newly introduced JS Frameworks based on UI components. This made the process easier and shorter to produce modern websites, with extra features such as re-using any component on our website. React thus was born (by Facebook), and today it's simply the most popular JS framework around.

What is React state management?

Each React component have a built-in state object, where you store data/assets that are persistent between component renderings. We can manage any component state locally or using an external state management library.

Local state management

Throughout this part of the series, We'll talk about managing our state locally.

Whilst React ships with many useful hooks, one of the most widely used ones is the useState() hook.

Well first, what is a hook exactly? From the official docs, a Hook is a special function that lets you hook into React features. useState is a Hook that lets you add React state to function components.

Example #1

Lets say we have a button that a user could toggle to show/hide specific data. In order to persist the status of this button, we have to use the useState hook to keep track of the state itself. Let's see the example below:

import {useState} from 'react'

export default function App() {

    // Declare a new state variable, which we'll call "isVisible"
    // It could be of any type, but here its a Boolean

    const [isVisible, setIsVisible] = useState(false)

    return (
        <>
            <button onClick={setIsVisible(prev => !prev)}>Toggle</button>
            {isVisible && <div>Hello there my friends, again!!</div>}
        </>
    )
}

First, when we called the useState hook, we declared a new "state variable". It returns a pair of values: the current state variable, which is in our example isVisible, and a function that we must use in order to update this value, aka setIsVisible in our example. The argument we passed to the hook itself is the initial state, which in our example represents a Boolean (false).

Next we hooked our button with a callback function, calling the setIsVisible(prev => !prev). Clicking on this button would effectively flip this Boolean value to the opposite of its previous value.

Thus, when a user presses the toggle button for the first time, it changes the isVisible boolean value from false to true, which results in showing the message <div>Hello there my friends, again!!</div>. Clicking it again results in hiding the value again as it was originally.

Example #2

Now let's take another example:

import {useState} from 'react'

const Message = ({user}) => {
    return (
        <h1>User Name: {user.name}</h1>
        <h2>User Age: {user.age}<h2>
    )
}

export default function App() {

    const [user, setUser] = useState({
        name: 'Sam',
        age: '24'
    })

    return (
        <>
            <p>User information:</p>
            <Message user={user} >
        </div>
    )
}

In the above example, In the App component (Parent) we declared a new state variable user using the useState hook, while passing the initial state as an object containing some user information (mimicking an object fetched from some auth API). We then called a child component Message that we 'll create now and passed the user state variable as a prop. Then we create this Message child component and destructure the user state variable from the props and consumed it as we want (in our example we just displayed user's name and age in a simple message).

This example is just a very simple and basic presentation on how state variables gets passed from a parent component to its child, which relates to prop-drilling. Whilst we should always keep in mind that this flow of data is always downwards, which means we simply can't declare a state variable in a child component and consume it in the parent, but its the other way around.

Downside of prop-drilling

Throughout a more complex development environment, sometimes simple prop-drilling of some state may create complexity to our work-flow. The reason is what if we need to consume a certain state variable not in a child directly, but rather in a deeper nested child. We'll need to tweak our code and find a better way of consuming state coming from a grand ancestor (grandparent). This is exactly where state management libraries come in hand. In the following parts of this blog series, we'll take a look at my implementations when dealing with global state management.

Final thoughts

State management is mandatory in any web development architecture, thus mastering your way through the best practices and ways of dealing with state management is a huge boost to your web development career, so please make sure to read the next upcoming parts of this blog series.

I'm constantly writing new blog posts where I share my expertise & skills in topics related to web development. If you're interested in such topics to boost your development career, consider following me! 😇 Or visit my personal website!

Thanks for reading, Ciao! 👋
Items.Author: iamshour

Items.Title: WTF 是 Kubernetes，作为 R 用户我应该关心吗？ | WTF is Kubernetes and Should I Care as R User?
Items.Description:
我将向您简要介绍 Kubernetes 以及如何使您的 R 工作在 Kubernetes 中大放异彩。

你是，

  • 使用 kubernetes 的公司的 R 用户？
  • 构建 R 应用程序（进行预测的模型、闪亮的应用程序、API）？
  • 对您的同事正在谈论的整个 Kubernetes 问题感到好奇吗？
  • 有点害怕？

那我有帖子给你！

许多 R 用户来自学术背景、统计学和社会科学。 这使您成为出色的问题解决者，对问题有深入的了解，并对世界有细致入微的理解。 你真的知道你在说什么！

但是有一个不同的世界，这个世界最重要的是向尽可能多的人展示广告。 我是在开玩笑，但计算机科学世界是 kubernetes 之类的想法诞生的地方。 和其他所有群体一样，使用的特定词在没有上下文的情况下很难理解。 这意味着您必须使用这些词来找到问题的答案。 这篇文章将介绍其中的一些词，我在底部有一个列表。 现在，我们所有人都可以通过您所在地区的云提供商使用它。

不要尝试自己设置 Kubernetes

让我从一个警告开始：如果您有一个模型需要在生产中运行，并且您周围没有其他基础架构人员可以提供帮助，请不要尝试设置 KUBERNETES！ 那是疯狂。 将代码打包到 docker 容器中相对容易，公共云有很多方法可以只运行 1 个容器。 管理和设置 kubernetes 集群需要做很多工作（有人告诉我，我是 kubernetes 等平台的快乐用户，但我不想管理它，到目前为止还没有）。

警告不碍事，我们走吧！

叉子是kubernetes。

当您第一次阅读 Kubernetes 时，您可能会觉得这里有这个人：

我假设您至少制作了一次 docker 图像（如果没有，我真的可以推荐摇杆图像！）。 您可能认为在您的操作系统中安装一个操作系统很烦人，但这个小型操作系统是一个您可以提供给其他人的系统，他们将能够像您一样运行它。 它是可重现的，并打包成一大块。 把这个想法更进一步，我们就到了 Kubernetes。

在 kubernetes （或拼写为 k8s，因为那更酷！）我们没有单个容器，而是有一组容器来构建我们的应用程序。 例如：数据库、负载均衡器、运行网站的代码，甚至运行预测代码的 R 容器，都可以成为电子商务网站。

您可能想知道为什么要为您的应用程序构建一个完整的 docker 容器负载，看起来很多重复的计算机没有充分的理由。 像这样构建它有充分的理由，但在我解释之前，让我们退一步看一下集群。

有大量的教程和 youtuber 将详细解释什么是 kubernetes。 我可以谈论计算和存储的抽象，但我不会。

我的看法是： kubernetes 控制容器。 你通过给它一个配置来告诉集群你想要什么。 这就像集群“看到”没有您指定类型的容器，因此它认为“我可能应该启动两个容器”。

如果出现问题，kubernetes 会纠正这种情况。

哦，kubernetes 并没有乱来，如果有什么东西崩溃了，整个容器都会被扔掉…… 并替换为新副本。

从组件构建复杂系统

我觉得 Kubernetes 有趣的一件事是，它允许您从更简单的部分配置复杂的应用程序：例如，一个负载均衡器容器、一个机器学习 API、一个数据库和三个网站服务容器。 所有这些都连接在一起形成了一个完整的业务。

因为应用程序都是独立的容器，您可以根据需要将它们换掉。 如果一家公司变得更大，您可以为该业务的那一部分分配一个团队，并且他们为该应用程序的那一部分所做的任何事情都不会影响其他部分。

您还可以在非常繁忙时动态增加为流量提供服务的容器数量，并在流量较少时缩减规模。

或者，您可以在不影响其他组件的情况下添加新版本的机器学习 API。 或者同时运行两个版本，其中一个提供流量，另一个在影子模式下运行，以针对旧版本测试您的新预测。

无论您想使用 R、python、matlab、julia 还是 javascript 进行工作，它都不会影响其他容器。 只要您的容器可以以标准方式与其他组件进行通信，您使用什么语言都没有关系。 在许多情况下，通过 REST API。 因此，如果您打包您的 R 代码并在顶部添加一个管道工 API，它就可以工作！

因此，多台小型独立计算机的想法通过这种方式变得更具吸引力。 您可以独立开发而不会干扰其他组件。 因此，如果您与网站团队一起工作，并且您负责将由网站调用的机器学习端点，您可以在相同的环境中运行该容器而不会出现任何问题。

每个 Kubernetes 部署（用于持续运行的容器的精美配置文件）或作业（用于在作业完成时启动和结束的容器的精美配置文件）都由配置和容器组成。 通常在 Dockerfile 中描述。 所以你的项目看起来像：

  • 你的代码（python/R/julia/matlab，无论你喜欢什么）
  • 打包代码的 Dockerfile
  • 一个配置文件（deployment.yaml，job.yaml）（有时其他人会为你做这件事）

所以你构建你的机器学习代码。 你将它打包到一个 docker 容器中（我们现在称它为镜像，因为我们很喜欢）。 您将该图像推送到注册表中。 使用配置文件，您可以告诉 kubernetes 集群在哪里可以找到该映像以及如何从中构建您的服务（“制作两个副本并为它们提供大量内存”）。 它有效！

成为有效的团队成员

那么，在使用 Kubernetes 时，如何才能成为一名有效的团队成员。

  1. 确保您的容器实际运行，广泛测试！
  2. 安排你的 API 应该是什么样子：当你使用{plumber}时：将调用什么端点，应该到达什么端口，以及数据是什么样的？ 确保为此编写一些测试！ 当您使用{shiny}时：它位于哪个端口上，您的应用程序的内存和 CPU 要求是什么。 在所有情况下：必须向容器提供哪些机密？
  3. 安排日志的去向和外观。 我最喜欢的 R 日志记录包是{logger} ，它可以执行多种形式的日志记录。 如果出现问题，您希望日志告诉您发生了什么以及您应该在哪里进行调查。

升级您的游戏并保护自己

这是一个相当复杂的机器，即使您只负责自己的容器。 因此，尽快发现故障非常重要。 如果您的预测机器因为您忘记安装 {purrr} 而无法工作，那将是一种耻辱。

以下内容是使您的工作更好的通用最佳实践。 更好、可重复且更可预测。 这意味着更少的惊喜。 因为惊喜是伟大的，除非有人在 03:00 时叫你起床。 如果您必须对关键应用程序进行紧急故障排除，因为您的项目死亡并且重新启动不起作用，并且以前的版本也不起作用，那么这很糟糕。 没有人喜欢这种惊喜。

您不必一次完成所有事情，每一步都是进步。 您希望确信您的工作符合所有标准。 在理想的世界中，您应该无所畏惧地部署新版本的代码，因为您确信错误会被自动步骤捕获。

因此，要尽快失败，在您的工作到达外部世界（生产环境）并让迭代超快之前，您至少需要测试您的代码。

测试一切

编写 R 测试来检查您是否可以处理预期的输入。 检查您是否在错误发生时记录错误。 检查你如何处理意外输入（一个 200 岁的人，一辆没有重量的汽车等）。 您测试您的 R 工作，因此您确信您的 R 代码可以正常工作。 您还必须测试容器，将预期输入传递给容器，传递意外输入，测试容器是否失败并在找不到所需的环境变量时大声抗议。

钉你能做的

修复可能会发生变化的事情：固定依赖项以使其在您的机器上工作，以及 docker 映像。 使用{renv}安装特定版本的 r-packages，并将其记录在锁定文件中。 也使用固定的基础容器，例如rocker/r-ver:4.0.0 。 如果你固定包版本和 docker 镜像，你的构建将是可重现的，这意味着更少的惊喜。 少一些惊喜对每个人都有好处。

自动化所有枯燥的步骤

我非常懒惰，重复的步骤让我流泪。 无聊的步骤让我忘记了关键步骤。

确保为您完成所有正确的操作。 编写这些步骤的脚本。 在您的版本控制系统中，执行检查代码样式和愚蠢事情（linting）的步骤，执行运行所有测试的步骤，执行构建 docker 映像的步骤，执行检查图像的步骤，执行步骤将映像推送到注册表，执行将新版本的代码部署到生产集群的步骤。

让计算机完成所有枯燥的步骤，如果自动化的东西通过了，你应该相信你的工作是有效的。 如果您没有信心，请认真思考为什么，是否存在您害怕的边缘情况？ 将它们添加到测试套件中。

我希望你从这篇文章中走出来，认为kubernetes 只是一堆 docker 镜像，有一个过分热心的老板，对集群中进行的所有工作进行微观管理，或者类似的东西。

你不应该太关心 kubernetes，我希望你周围有一个基础设施团队，可以帮助你自动化 kubernetes 的基本部分。 只需确保您的 docker 镜像正常工作，并与您周围的现有应用程序很好地集成。

我也希望你能去那个基础设施团队，用正确的词来寻求帮助。 随着时间的推移，我希望你是一个无所畏惧的部署者（新词），用你令人敬畏的 R 产品震撼世界，我希望听到你在会议上谈论这项令人敬畏的工作。

笔记

行话和花哨的词

有时，寻求帮助最困难的部分是找到合适的词来描述您的问题。

  • 容器注册表：kubernetes 集群必须从注册表中提取容器，因此您的镜像最终进入该注册表至关重要。
  • 这里有一个明确的航海主题。 有一种方法可以使用 'helm' 在 Kubernetes 上部署整个应用程序，并且有一个称为 'harbor' 的 docker 注册表。


原文:

I’m going to give you a high overview of kubernetes and how you can make your R work shine in kubernetes.

Are you,

  • an R-user in a company that uses kubernetes?
  • building R applications (models that do predictions, shiny applications, APIs)?
  • curious about this whole kubernetes thing that your coworkers are talking about?
  • somewhat afraid?

Then I have the post for you!

Many R users come from an academic background, statistics and social sciences. That makes you an excellent problem solver with a deep knowledge of problems and a nuanced understanding of the world. You actually know what you are talking about!

But there is a different world, a world where the most important thing is showing an advertisement to as many people as possible. I’m joking, but the computer science world is where ideas like kubernetes were born. And like every other group specific words are used that can be hard to understand without context. That means that you have to use those words to find answers on your questions. This post will introduce some of those words and I have a list at the bottom. And now it is available to all of us, through a cloud provider in your area.

Don’t try to setup kubernetes yourself

Let me start with a warning: if you have one model that needs to run in production and there is no other infrastructure people around you to help, DO NOT TRY TO SETUP KUBERNETES! That is madness. It is relatively easy to package up your code into a docker container and the public cloud has many ways to run just 1 container. Managing and setting up a kubernetes cluster is a lot of work (So I’ve been told, I am a happy user of platforms like kubernetes but I do not want to manage it, and so far have not).

Warnings out of the way, let’s go!

What the fork is kubernetes.

When you first read about kubernetes you might feel like this person here:

I assume you have made a docker image at least once (if not I can really recommend the rocker images!). Docker is like a small operating system that you install your stuff on. You might think it is annoying to have an operating system inside your operating system but this small operating system is a system you can give to someone else and they will be able to run it exactly like you. It’s reproducible and packaged up in one chunk. Take that idea one step further and we get to kubernetes.

In kubernetes (or spelled k8s, because that is way cooler!) we don’t have a single container, but a fleet of containers to build our applications. For instance: a database, a load balancer, the code that runs a website and maybe even your R container that runs your prediction code combined can become an e-commerce site.

You might wonder why you would want to build a whole load of docker containers for your applications, it seems like a lot of duplicated computers for no good reason. There are good reasons to build it like this, but before I explain, let’s step back and look at the cluster.

Kubernetes orchestrates containers

There are a gazillion tutorials and youtubers that will explain in details what kubernetes is. I could talk about abstractions of compute and storage, but I will not.

The way I see it is: kubernetes controls containers. You tell the cluster what you want by giving it a configuration. Kubernetes controls your containers by comparing that configuration of what you want: “two versions of this container with x amount of cpu” against what it currently has “no containers of this kind”. Kubernetes will take actions to move towards that state. It’s like the cluster ‘sees’ that there are no containers of the type you specified and so it thinks “I should probably start two containers”.

The kubernetes cluster continuously monitors the state of the cluster against its instructions.

And if something is off, kubernetes corrects the situation.

Oh and kubernetes is not messing around, if something is crashing the entire container will be thrown out … and replaced by a fresh copy.

Building complex systems from components

One of the things I find interesting about kubernetes is that it allows you to configure a complex application from simpler parts: one load balancer container, one machine learning API, one database, and three website serving containers for example. All wired up together form an entire business.

Because the applications are all separate containers you can swap them out if you want to. If a company gets larger you can allocate one team for that one part of the business and whatever they do for that part of the application does not influence other parts.

You can also dynamically increase the number of containers that serve traffic when it is very busy, and downscale when there is less traffic.

Or you can add a new version of your machine learning API without impacting the other components. Or run two versions at the same time where one is serving traffic and the other is running in shadow mode to test your new predictions against the old one.

Whether you want to use R, python, matlab, julia or javascript for your work, it does not affect the other containers at all. It does not matter at all what language you use, as long as your container can communicate in a standard way with other components. In many cases through a REST API. So if you package up your R code and add a plumber API on top it just works!

So the idea of multiple small separate computers becomes more attractive this way. You can develop independently without interfering other components. So if you work together with a website team and you are responsible for a Machine Learning endpoint that will be called by the website you can run that container in the same environment with very little problems.

Kubernetes in practice

Every kubernetes deployment (fancy config file for a continuously running container) or job (fancy config file for a container that starts and finishes when the job is done) consists of configuration and a container. Usually described in a Dockerfile. So your project looks like:

  • your code (python/R/julia/matlab, whatever you fancy)
  • a Dockerfile that packages up your code
  • a configuration file (deployment.yaml, job.yaml) (sometimes someone else will do this for you)

So you build your machine learning code. You package it up into a docker container (we call it an image now, because we are fancy). You push that image into a registry. Using the configuration file, you tell the kubernetes cluster where to find that image and how to build your service out of it (“make two copies and give them lots of ram”). Kubernetes has new instructions now, and makes sure the cluster state matches those instructions. And it works!

Being an effective team member

So how can you be an effective team member when working with kubernetes.

  1. make sure your container actually runs, test that extensively!
  2. arrange what your API should look like: when you use {plumber}: what endpoint will be called, what port should be reached for, and what will the data look like? Make sure you write some tests for that! When you use {shiny}: what port does it live on, what are the memory and CPU requirements for your application. In all cases: what secrets must be supplied to the container?
  3. arrange where logs should go and how they should look. My favorite R logging package is {logger} and it can do many many forms of logging. If something goes wrong you want the logs to tell you what happened and where you should investigate.

Upgrading your game and protecting yourself

This is quite a complex machinery, even if you are only responsible for your own container. So it’s important that failures are caught as quickly as possible. It would be a shame if your prediction machine does not work because you forgot to install {purrr}.

The following things are generic best practices that make your work better. Better, reproducible and more predictable. That means less surprises. BEcause surprises are great, except when someone calls you out of your bed at 03:00 hours. It sucks if you have to emergency troubleshoot a critical application because your project died and restarting doesn’t work, nor does a previous version work. No one likes that kind of surprise.

You don’t have to implement everything all at once, every step is progress. You want to be confident your work meets all the standards. In an ideal world, you should be fearless in deploying new versions of your code, because you are confident that mistakes will be caught by automated steps.

So to fail as quickly as possible, before your work reaches the outside world (production) and make the iterations superfast you need to at least test your code.

Test everything

Write R tests that check if you can handle the expected inputs. Check that you are logging errors when they occur. Check how you handle unexpected inputs (a person with an age of 200, a car with no weight, etc). You test your R work so you are confident that your R code works. You also have to test the container, pass expected input to the container, pass unexpected input, test if the container fails and protests loudly when the required environmental variables are not found.

Pin what you can

Fix things that can vary: pin dependencies so that it works on your machine, as well as a docker image. Use {renv} to install specific versions of r-packages, and to record that in a lockfile. Use pinned base containers too, for instance rocker/r-ver:4.0.0. If you pin the package versions and docker images your build will be reproducible, and that means less surprises. Less surprises is good for everyone.

Automate all the dull steps

I’m extremely lazy, repetitive steps bore me to tears. And boring steps makes me forget critical steps.

Make sure all the right actions are done for you. Script those steps. In your version control system, make steps that check your code on style and stupid things (linting), make a step that runs all the tests, make a step that builds the docker image, make a step that checks the image, make a step that pushes the image to the registry, make a step that deploys the new version of your code to the production cluster.

Make the computer do all the dull steps, if the automated stuff passes, you should be confident your work works. If you do not feel confident, think hard about why, are there edgecases that you fear? Add them to the testsuite.

I hope you come away from this post with the idea that kubernetes is just a bunch of docker images with an overzealous boss that micromanages all the work that goes on in a cluster , or something like that.

You should not care that much about kubernetes, I hope you have an infrastructure team around you that will help you automate the essentials parts of kubernetes. Just make sure your docker image works, and integrates nicely with the existing applications around you.

I also hope you can go to that infrastructure team and ask for help using the right words. In time I hope you are a fearless deployer (new word) that rocks the world with your awesome R-product, and I hope to hear you speak about that awesome work on a conference.

Notes

Jargon and fancy words

Sometimes the hardest part of finding help is finding the right words to describe your problem.

  • image : although there are differences between an image and container (=active image) in practice it does not matter that much in communication
  • container registry : The kubernetes cluster must pull the containers from a registry, so it is vital your image ends up in that register.
  • k8s : the cool way to spell kubernetes (I don’t know how you can make /coo-ber-net-ees/ from k-eight-s; but it did allow for k9s as interface and k3s for a mini kubernetes on your computer)
  • kubernetes , it’s an american pronounciation of the greek word ‘κυβερνήτης’ which means helmsman. There is a clear nautical theme here. Docker has an image of a whale, the concept of multiple docker containers with some control around it is known as a ‘pod’. There is a way to deploy entire applications on kubernetes with ‘helm’ and there is a docker registry known as ‘harbor’.
Items.Author: rmhogervorst

Items.Title: 使用 Lightrun 调试 Java 消息服务 (JMS) API | Debugging the Java Message Service (JMS) API using Lightrun
Items.Description:
这对于高吞吐量系统非常有用——企业应用程序可以发送消息，而不是通过实时执行缓慢的操作来浪费用户时间。 这种非阻塞方法可实现极高的吞吐量，同时保持大规模的可靠性。

该消息带有一个事务上下文，它为可传递性和可靠性提供了一些保证。 因此，我们可以在方法中发布一条消息，然后直接返回，这与我们在写入 ACID 数据库时所拥有的保证类似。

我们可以认为消息传递有点像社区邮件列表。 您向代表特定列表的电子邮件地址发送消息。 订阅该列表的每个人都会收到该消息。 在这种情况下，消息主题代表社区邮件列表地址。 您可以向其发布消息，Java 消息服务处理程序可以使用消息侦听器来接收所述事件。

重要的是要注意 JMS 中有两种消息传递模型：发布和订阅模型（我们在此讨论）和点对点消息传递，它允许您将消息发送到特定目的地。

让我们快速演示一下。

一个简单的演示

为了调试 Java 消息服务调用，我创建了一个简单的演示应用程序，可以在此处找到其源代码。

这个 JMS 演示是一个简单的数据库日志 API——它是一个微服务，您可以使用它来发布日志条目，然后将其异步写入数据库。 然后，RESTful 应用程序可以使用此数据库日志 API 添加数据库日志条目，而无需数据库访问的开销。

此代码实现了主要的 Web 服务：

 @RestController @RequiredArgsConstructor public class EventRequest { private final JmsTemplate jmsTemplate ; private final EventService eventService ; private final Moshi moshi = new Moshi . Builder (). build (); @PostMapping ( "/add" ) public void event ( @RequestBody EventDTO event ) { String json = moshi . adapter ( EventDTO . class ). toJson ( event ); jmsTemplate . send ( "event" , session -> session . createTextMessage ( json )); } @GetMapping ( "/list" ) public List < EventDTO > listEvents () { return eventService . listEvents (); } }

请注意将消息发布到事件主题的event()方法。 为了简单起见，我之前没有讨论消息正文，但请注意，在这种情况下，我只是将 JSON 字符串作为正文传递。 虽然 JMS 支持对象序列化，但使用该功能有其自身的复杂性，我希望保持代码简单。

为了补充主要的 Web 服务，我们需要构建一个处理传入消息的侦听器：

 @Component @RequiredArgsConstructor public class EventListener { private final EventService eventService ; private final Moshi moshi = new Moshi . Builder (). build (); @JmsListener ( destination = "event" ) public void handleMessage ( String eventDTOJSON ) throws IOException { eventService . storeEvent ( moshi . adapter ( EventDTO . class ). fromJson ( eventDTOJSON )); } }

使用发送到侦听器的 JSON 字符串调用侦听器，我们对其进行解析并将其发送到服务。

调试隐藏代码

不幸的是，这种类型的面向消息的中间件隐藏了许多脆弱的实现细节，这些细节可能会在此过程中失败。

这在生产场景中尤其痛苦，因为消息没有正确发送而很难知道问题是否发生。 这就是 Lightrun 的用武之地。

您可以将 Lightrun 操作（快照、日志等）直接放入平台 API 和消息传递服务的实现中。 这让我们可以确定消息选择器是否按预期工作以及消息侦听器是否确实被触发。

如上图所示，使用带有 JMS 支持的 Spring，我们可以打开JmsTemplate并将快照添加到 execute 方法：

如您所见，发送到主题时会调用该操作。 我们可以查看堆栈帧以查看接收消息的主题并使用条件来缩小正确的消息处理程序。

我们可以在消息源中放置一个匹配的快照，以便我们可以跟踪流。 例如， EventRequest中的快照可以为我们提供一些见解。 我们也可以朝另一个方向挖掘。

在上面的堆栈中，您可以看到 execute 方法由方法 send 在第 584 行调用。execute 方法包装了调用者，因此操作将是异步的。 我们可以通过转到闭包并在那里放置快照来进一步向下堆栈：

请注意，在这里我们可以对特定主题设置条件并缩小范围。

我们选择消息传递系统以使我们的应用程序可靠。 但是，企业消息传递系统很难在生产环境中调试，这不利于这种可靠性。 我们可以在消息的目标中看到日志，但是如果我们没有到达它会发生什么？

使用 Lightrun，我们可以将操作放置在基于消息传递的应用程序的所有不同层中。 无论消息标准或平台如何，这都有助于我们缩小问题范围。 您可以使用免费套餐完成所有这些工作。



原文:

The Java Message Service API (JMS) was developed by Sun Microsystems in the days of Java EE. The JMS API provides us with simple messaging abstractions including Message Producer, Message Consumer, etc. Messaging APIs let us place a message on a “queue” and consume messages placed into said queue. This is immensely useful for high throughput systems – instead of wasting user time by performing a slow operation in real-time, an enterprise application can send a message. This non-blocking approach enables extremely high throughput, while maintaining reliability at scale.

The message carries a transactional context which provides some guarantees on deliverability and reliability. As a result, we can post a message in a method and then just return, which provides similar guarantees to the ones we have when writing to an ACID database.

We can think of messaging somewhat like a community mailing list. You send a message to an email address which represents a specific list. Everyone who subscribes to that list receives that message. In this case, the message topic represents the community mailing list address. You can post a message to it, and the Java Message Service handler can use a message listener to receive said event.

It’s important to note that there are two messaging models in JMS: the publish-and-subscribe model (which we discussed here) and also point-to-point messaging, which lets you send a message to a specific destination.

Let’s go over a quick demo.

A Simple Demo

In order to debug the Java Message Service calls, I’ve created a simple demo application, whose source code can be found here.

This JMS demo is a simple database log API – it’s a microservice which you can use to post a log entry, which is then written to the database asynchronously. RESTful applications can then use this database log API to add a database log entry and without the overhead of database access.

This code implements the main web service:

@RestController
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class EventRequest { private final JmsTemplate jmsTemplate; private final EventService eventService; private final Moshi moshi = new Moshi.Builder().build(); @PostMapping("/add") public void event(@RequestBody EventDTO event) { String json = moshi.adapter(EventDTO.class).toJson(event); jmsTemplate.send("event", session -> session.createTextMessage(json)); } @GetMapping("/list") public List<EventDTO> listEvents() { return eventService.listEvents(); }
}

Notice the event() method that posts a message to the event topic. I didn’t discuss message bodies before to keep things simple, but note that in this case I just pass a JSON string as the body. While JMS supports object serialization, using that capability has its own complexities and I want to keep the code simple.

To complement the main web service, we’d need to build a listener that handles the incoming message:

@Component
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class EventListener { private final EventService eventService; private final Moshi moshi = new Moshi.Builder().build(); @JmsListener(destination = "event") public void handleMessage(String eventDTOJSON) throws IOException { eventService.storeEvent(moshi.adapter(EventDTO.class).fromJson(eventDTOJSON)); }
}

The listener is invoked with the JSON string that is sent to the listener, which we parse and send on to the service.

Debugging the Hidden Code

The great thing about abstractions like Spring and JMS is that you don’t need to write a lot of boilerplate code. Unfortunately, message-oriented middleware of this type hides a lot of fragile implementation details that can fail along the way.

This is especially painful in a production scenario where it’s hard to know whether the problem occurred because a message wasn’t sent properly. This is where Lightrun comes in.

You can place Lightrun actions (snapshots, logs etc.) directly into the platform APIs and implementations of messaging services. This lets us determine if message selectors are working as expected and whether the message listener is indeed triggered.

With Spring with JMS support as shown above, we can open the JmsTemplate and add a snapshot to the execute method:

As you can see, the action is invoked when sending to a topic. We can review the stack frame to see the topic that receives the message and use conditions to narrow down the right handler for messages.

We can place a matching snapshot in the source of message so we can track the flow. E.g. a snapshot in EventRequest can provide us with some insight. We can dig in the other direction too.

In the stack above, you can see that the execute method is invoked by the method send at line 584. The execute method wraps the caller so the operation will be asynchronous. We can go further down the stack by going to the closure and placing a snapshot there:

Notice that here we can place a condition on the specific topic and narrow things down.

TL;DR

We pick messaging systems to make our application reliable. However, enterprise messaging systems are very hard to debug in production, which works against that reliability. We can see logs in the target of messages, but what happens if we did not reach it?

With Lightrun we can place actions in all the different layers of messaging-based applications. This helps us narrow down the problem regardless of the messaging standard or platform. You can accomplish all of this using the free tier.

Items.Title: 什么是无服务器？ | What is Serverless?
Items.Description:
发表于基本上在这个概念中，开发人员不需要管理服务器。 他们只是部署代码和功能。

无服务器并不意味着没有服务器，而是意味着作为开发人员您无法管理/提供/查看它们。

例如 Amazon S3、DynamoDB、Fargate、Lambda 等都是无服务器服务。

帕维尔·祖布凯维奇—— Jan 24 1月24日

Wojciech Matuszewski - Wojciech Matuszewski - Jan 24 1月24日

Turja Narayan Chaudhuri - Turja Narayan Chaudhuri - Jan 24 1月24日



原文:

Md Shahriyar Al Mustakim Mitul

Posted on May 2

Basically in this concept, developers don't need to manage servers. They just deploy codes, and functions.

Serverless does not mean that there is no server, rather it means that as a developer you can not manage/provision/see them.

For example Amazon S3, DynamoDB, Fargate, Lambda, etc. are serverless services.
